highlights: Conan was caught holding a nail clipper mid-interview and Tanner somehow found this iconic

Avalanche Terrain.summary: Conan interviews Tanner, a ski patroller and avalanche safety expert from Idaho, drilling into the technical and psychological realities of working in terrain where a single mistake can be fatal. Rather than treating avalanches as abstract disaster material, Conan pushes Tanner to describe actual close calls and the calculus of deciding when a mountain is too dangerous to patrol.

highlights_v2.title: How Tanner became avalanche certified
highlights_v2.summary: Tanner explains the grueling training process, including deliberate burial exercises, that ski patrollers undergo to learn how to read terrain and predict collapse before it happens.
highlights_v2.notable_quote: You have to understand the snowpack like you understand your own body.

highlights_v2.title: Living with constant life-or-death decisions
highlights_v2.summary: Conan asks what it feels like to make judgment calls that directly determine whether you or your team goes home alive, and Tanner describes the mental toll of that responsibility.
highlights_v2.notable_quote: By the time you're 30, you've made thousands of those calls. You never stop second-guessing yourself.

highlights_v2.title: The moment Tanner nearly died on the job
highlights_v2.summary: Tanner recounts a specific incident where conditions shifted beneath him and he narrowly escaped a slab that would have buried him completely.
highlights_v2.notable_quote: I heard it crack. That sound—you never forget it.

highlights_v2.title: Why people underestimate avalanche danger
highlights_v2.summary: Conan asks why skiers and backcountry enthusiasts keep dying despite warnings, and Tanner breaks down the psychology of risk denial versus the actual math of survival.
highlights_v2.notable_quote: Most people think they're the exception. The mountain doesn't care if you think you're the exception.

highlights_v2.title: Working from Home Without Home

highlights_v2.title: The Noise-to-Solitude Ratio

highlights_v2.title: Cultural Norm Meets American Puzzlement

We Have A Condom For That.conanResponse: Conan said yes, absolutely, and expressed deep personal regret that this technology wasn't available when he was younger, before catching himself and changing the subject.
We Have A Condom For That.summary: Conan interviews Noah, a condom size specialist from Massachusetts, who walks him through the surprisingly rigorous science behind proper condom fit, measurements, percentile data, and the common misconceptions that lead people to choose the wrong size. The conversation oscillates between Conan's genuine curiosity about the technical aspects and his amusement at having stumbled onto a legitimate, unglamorous expertise.

highlights_v2.title: The scientific rigor of condom sizing
highlights_v2.summary: Noah explains that condom fit is determined by precise measurements, length, width, and nominal width, with data based on percentile distributions, the same methodology used in medical device engineering.
highlights_v2.notable_quote: It's all about percentiles and nominal width—this isn't guess-and-check.

highlights_v2.title: Why people buy the wrong size
highlights_v2.summary: Noah reveals that most people either choose based on ego or embarrassment, not actual fit, leading to condoms that are either too loose or too tight, defeating their purpose entirely.
highlights_v2.notable_quote: Men buy larger than they need, women buy smaller—it's all psychology, not anatomy.

highlights_v2.title: Conan's dawning realization
highlights_v2.summary: Conan realizes he's having an earnest conversation about condom dimensions with a specialist, oscillating between treating it as genuine health information and barely suppressing laughter at the absurdity of the topic.
highlights_v2.notable_quote: So you're telling me there's literally a chart for this.

highlights_v2.title: The condom fit problem nobody talks about
highlights_v2.summary: Noah discusses how improper fit leads to breakage, slippage, and reduced effectiveness, a public health issue disguised as an embarrassing conversation nobody wants to have.
highlights_v2.notable_quote: A condom that doesn't fit right is basically useless, but nobody admits they bought the wrong size.

The House Dick.summary: Conan speaks with Trisha, a hotel detective in the Philippines who manages security and operations for a small hotel. highlights_v2.title: The actual job of a house dick

highlights_v2.title: Running a small hotel in the Philippines

highlights_v2.title: Strange guest situations and theft

highlights_v2.title: Conan's curiosity about weird hotel stories

highlights: Josh co-founded a company selling direct-to-consumer caskets and urns — cutting out the funeral home markup
The casket industry's pricing turned out to be even more eyebrow-raising than Conan had expected
Josh believes that shopping for your own casket is actually an act of love for your family
Putting The Fun Back In Funeral.fanQuestion: Have you given any thought to what you want for your own funeral?
Putting The Fun Back In Funeral.conanResponse: Conan said he wants something 'theatrical and confusing,' possibly involving his body being launched from a catapult while an orchestra plays, to ensure everyone is too bewildered to grieve.
Putting The Fun Back In Funeral.summary: Josh founded a direct-to-consumer casket and urn company, and Conan spends the call probing the actual economics and logistics of selling death boxes online, how you market them, who your customers are, whether people impulse-buy caskets. The conversation stays grounded in the mundane business reality of the funeral industry rather than drifting into morbidity.

highlights_v2.title: Direct-to-consumer casket pitch
highlights_v2.summary: Josh explains the value proposition: funeral homes mark up caskets 300–400%, so his company cuts out the middleman and lets families buy directly at wholesale prices.
highlights_v2.notable_quote: Funeral homes were charging families thousands more than the cost to make it—we wanted to democratize that.

highlights_v2.title: How do you advertise caskets online
highlights_v2.summary: Conan zeroes in on the advertising problem: nobody wakes up wanting to shop for caskets, so Josh walks through the practical reality of reaching grieving families at the exact moment they need one.
highlights_v2.notable_quote: When someone's lost a loved one and they're searching, that's when they find us—not browsing for fun.

highlights_v2.title: The casket as a commodity product
highlights_v2.summary: Conan asks whether caskets have become commodified like any other consumer good, and Josh acknowledges the emotional weight families place on the decision despite the product being functionally identical across price points.
highlights_v2.notable_quote: It's wood or metal—the function is the same, but people still want to feel like they're honoring their loved one.

highlights_v2.title: Scale and volume of the funeral industry
highlights_v2.summary: Conan asks what percentage of U.S. deaths his company handles and gets a sense of the sheer volume of the market, millions of burials annually, which Josh positions as a massive opportunity for disruption.
highlights_v2.notable_quote: There are nearly three million deaths a year in America—the funeral industry is huge and mostly offline.

highlights_v2.title: Josh's personal entry into the casket business
highlights_v2.summary: Josh reveals why he started the company, a personal experience with a family funeral that made him aware of the markup, sparking the idea to offer a more transparent alternative.
highlights_v2.notable_quote: When my grandmother passed, I saw firsthand how much funeral homes were charging—that's when the lightbulb went off.

highlights_v2.title: The Marital Happiness Spreadsheet

highlights_v2.title: What Gets Measured in Marriage

highlights_v2.title: ER Doctor Logic Meets Romance

highlights: Stelios explained the Greek philosophy of relaxation — which is essentially an entire way of life

Hot Athens.summary: Conan calls Stelios, a lifestyle coach from Athens, to learn the Greek philosophy behind doing absolutely nothing. Stelios walks Conan through the cultural practice of taking time to slow down, sit with friends, and let the day happen, a concept that visibly confuses someone whose entire career is built on perpetual productivity.

highlights_v2.title: The Greek art of doing nothing
highlights_v2.summary: Stelios explains that Greek relaxation isn't about meditation apps or structured wellness, it's about sitting in a café with no agenda, which Conan struggles to grasp.
highlights_v2.notable_quote: We don't need to optimize the relaxation. We just sit.

highlights_v2.title: Conan's productivity anxiety
highlights_v2.summary: When Stelios describes hours spent at a taverna with friends and ouzo, Conan immediately asks what work gets done, revealing his inability to separate time from output.

Cocoland.conanResponse: Conan designed a theme park entirely dedicated to his own anxieties, where every ride ends ambiguously and the gift shop sells only things that are slightly wrong.
Cocoland.summary: Conan interviews Margaret, a theme park designer from Singapore, about the Haw Par Villa, a bizarre, decades-old amusement park filled with grotesque statuary, nightmarish dioramas, and inexplicable attractions that somehow became a genuine cultural landmark. Conan's curiosity pivots between genuine bewilderment at the park's aesthetic and Margaret's matter-of-fact explanations of its history and design philosophy.

highlights_v2.title: The Haw Par Villa's Nightmarish Diorama Design
highlights_v2.summary: Margaret describes the park's famous grotesque sculptures and surreal attractions, prompting Conan to ask increasingly specific questions about why the park's designers chose to populate it with disturbing imagery rather than traditional family-friendly theming.
highlights_v2.notable_quote: It's like someone said, 'What if a theme park had no rules and only followed its own internal logic?'

highlights_v2.title: Margaret Normalizes the Abnormal
highlights_v2.summary: As Conan expresses mounting disbelief at specific park features, Margaret calmly explains them as historical and cultural artifacts, maintaining that the Haw Par Villa's strangeness is entirely intentional and beloved by Singaporeans.
highlights_v2.notable_quote: This is normal to me—it's been here my whole life.

highlights_v2.title: Conan's Inability to Wrap His Head Around the Park
highlights_v2.summary: Conan repeatedly tries to categorize the Haw Par Villa within familiar theme park frameworks and repeatedly fails, leading him to ask Margaret clarifying questions that reveal the park operates on a completely different cultural and aesthetic logic.
highlights_v2.notable_quote: So it's terrifying, historically significant, and Singaporeans genuinely enjoy it?

highlights_v2.title: Theme Park Design as Cultural Expression
highlights_v2.summary: Margaret articulates how the Haw Par Villa represents a specific moment in Singapore's history and reflects values and aesthetics that don't translate to Western theme park conventions, causing Conan to appreciate the park as genuine rather than unintentionally comedic.
highlights_v2.notable_quote: The park is a time capsule of what we valued and how we saw the world.

highlights: Lucy is a jazz singer who specializes in scat — the wordless vocal improvisation technique
Conan attempted scat and the results were enthusiastically received and never spoken of again
Lucy explained that scat is essentially having a conversation with the band using only sounds
The Jazz Singer.fanQuestion: Do you have any secret musical talents, or is it all just being tall and pale?
The Jazz Singer.conanResponse: Conan claimed to have a deeply suppressed gift for jazz scat that had been crushed by his years of television, then demonstrated something that Lucy generously described as 'a start.'
The Jazz Singer.summary: Conan interviews Lucy, a jazz singer from New York City, about her career and vocal technique. The conversation centers on scat singing, the improvisational, rhythmic vocal style that defines modern jazz performance, as Lucy walks Conan through how she approaches the craft.

highlights_v2.title: Scat Singing Explained
highlights_v2.summary: Lucy breaks down vocal scat technique and how jazz singers use nonsense syllables to mimic instrumental improvisation.
highlights_v2.notable_quote: It's like your voice becomes another instrument—you're trading solos with the band.

highlights_v2.title: Learning to Scat
highlights_v2.summary: Lucy describes the trial-and-error process of developing her scat skills and how different jazz singers approach the same technique differently.
highlights_v2.notable_quote: Every singer sounds completely different when they scat—it's like a fingerprint.

highlights_v2.title: Performance Pressure
highlights_v2.summary: Lucy reveals the challenge of improvising vocally in front of audiences and how spontaneity can feel both liberating and terrifying.
highlights_v2.notable_quote: You never know what's going to come out of your mouth, and the band has to follow you.

highlights: Joey works to preserve rare and historically significant home movies, including some from famous families
The film collections he has worked with include footage that no living person has seen in decades
Conan was deeply invested in which celebrity home movies might exist and whether he could see them
12 Clomp Program.fanQuestion: If you could preserve one moment from your own life on film forever, what would it be?
12 Clomp Program.conanResponse: Conan said he'd preserve a quiet dinner with his family where nothing notable happened, which he claimed would be the most subversive thing he could possibly leave behind.
12 Clomp Program.summary: Conan interviews Joey, a film preservationist from Toronto who specializes in restoring and archiving famous home movie collections. The conversation centers on the technical and emotional dimensions of his work, how he decides what's worth saving, the deterioration he encounters, and what these intimate family records reveal about the people who filmed them.

highlights_v2.title: The weight of preserving family memories
highlights_v2.summary: Joey describes the emotional responsibility of handling irreplaceable home movies, knowing he's often the last chance to save footage before it deteriorates into nothing.
highlights_v2.notable_quote: You're holding someone's entire family history in your hands, literally on deteriorating film stock.

highlights_v2.title: What gets thrown away and why
highlights_v2.summary: Joey explains how countless home movie collections end up in estate sales or dumpsters because families don't realize they have archival value or don't know how to preserve them.
highlights_v2.notable_quote: People see a box of old film reels in the attic and think it's junk because they can't play it anymore.

highlights_v2.title: Conan's surprise at the technical complexity
highlights_v2.summary: Conan reacts with genuine surprise when Joey details the various film formats, decay patterns, and restoration equipment required, it's far more complex than casual viewers realize.
highlights_v2.notable_quote: Wait, so you actually need different machines for different types of old film? This is basically CSI for home movies.

highlights_v2.title: The strangest collections Joey has encountered
highlights_v2.summary: Joey recounts a memorable restoration project involving an unusual or unexpected home movie collection that challenged his assumptions about what families documented.
highlights_v2.notable_quote: You never know what you're going to find in those old reels—sometimes it's boring birthday parties, sometimes it's something you'd never expect someone to be filming.

highlights: Micah works in advertising design — specifically making fast food look irresistible in photos
Conan became surprisingly invested in designing his own signature fast food combo
The episode ended with a fully branded 'Conan Slab-a-Roo' meal concept and a demand for royalties
The Conan Slabaroo.fanQuestion: If Conan O'Brien had a custom drink or combo, what would it be?
The Conan Slabaroo.conanResponse: Conan said his combo had to be ham-based to honor his Irish heritage, eventually naming it 'The Conan Slab-a-Roo' — a large ham sandwich with fries and a drink — and insisted he should be paid hundreds of dollars for lending his name to any such product.
The Conan Slabaroo.summary: Conan calls Micah, an advertising designer from Kansas City, to explore the peculiar craft of making fast food look edible on camera. The conversation pivots between the technical trickery of food photography and Micah's own philosophy on selling something most people know tastes different in reality.

highlights_v2.title: The illusion of appetizing fast food
highlights_v2.summary: Conan asks Micah to explain how a burger photographed for an ad bears almost no resemblance to what arrives in a cardboard box, and Micah walks him through the honest gap between what looks good and what tastes good.
highlights_v2.notable_quote: You're basically lying for a living—but making a living doing it.

highlights_v2.title: Fake versus real ingredients on set
highlights_v2.summary: Micah reveals that professional food stylists use non-edible substances, melted plastic, motor oil substitutes, cotton balls, to make food look more appetizing than actual food ever could.
highlights_v2.notable_quote: The burger you see isn't a burger—it's a prop that lost a lawsuit.

highlights_v2.title: Micah's philosophical compromise
highlights_v2.summary: When pressed on the ethics of advertising something that looks nothing like the product, Micah explains his approach: make it aspirational, not deceptive, and trust that people understand the implicit deal with advertising.
highlights_v2.notable_quote: Everyone knows what they're getting. It's part of the game.

highlights_v2.title: Conan's genuine curiosity about the work
highlights_v2.summary: Rather than mining the topic for easy jokes about fast food being gross, Conan leans into authentic questions about Micah's day-to-day process, treating the craft of food advertising as legitimately interesting.
highlights_v2.notable_quote: So you're essentially a magician who makes french fries look like they deserve their own biopic.

highlights: Trisha covers real cold cases for a living — Conan found this simultaneously terrifying and cool
She has personally interviewed serial killers in prison as part of her investigative work
Conan walked away from this one with genuine respect and a slight wariness of Trisha
Bit@#es Be Hitchin'.fanQuestion: What advice would you give me the next time I go in and interview a serial killer?
Bit@#es Be Hitchin'.conanResponse: Conan advised finding common ground immediately — even comparing his own experience of 'killing' audiences through bad comedy to the killer's crimes — to build rapport that might eventually yield a confession.
Bit@#es Be Hitchin'.summary: Conan interviews Trisha, an investigative crime reporter from Minnesota, about the realities of chasing stories in criminal investigations, the danger, the obsession, and the moral weight of exposing wrongdoing. He pushes her on what drives someone to pursue this kind of work and whether the job has made her paranoid or hardened.

highlights_v2.title: Why crime reporting obsesses people
highlights_v2.summary: Trisha explains the psychological pull of investigative journalism, the addictive nature of uncovering hidden stories that people want buried.
highlights_v2.notable_quote: Once you start pulling on a thread, you can't stop—even when you probably should.

highlights_v2.title: Danger and close calls in the field
highlights_v2.summary: Trisha recounts a moment when pursuing a story put her in genuine physical danger, forcing her to confront the actual stakes of the work.
highlights_v2.notable_quote: I realized I was in a situation where nobody knew where I was.

highlights_v2.title: Does reporting crime change who you are
highlights_v2.summary: Conan presses Trisha on whether years of digging into murders and corruption has warped her view of humanity or made her fundamentally distrustful.
highlights_v2.notable_quote: You can't unsee what you've seen.

highlights_v2.title: The moral cost of exposure
highlights_v2.summary: Trisha reflects on the weight of knowing she's changing lives through her reporting, sometimes for good, sometimes creating collateral damage to innocent people caught in the story.
highlights_v2.notable_quote: Power without reflection is just cruelty with a byline.

highlights: Jon has webbed toes, which he apparently leads with as a fun fact in every social situation

highlights_v2.title: Vasco's Casual National Pride

highlights_v2.title: Conan Discovers History's Quiet Winners
highlights_v2.summary: Conan realizes that maintaining a stable border for centuries is actually a massive historical win that nobody talks about because it's boring, the anti-conquest story.

Zero Degrees Kelvin.summary: Conan calls Kelvin, a Dene Cultural Advocate from Canada's Far North, and immediately fixates on the episode title's ironic promise, that Kelvin's name somehow connects to absolute zero temperature, only to discover Kelvin has no idea his name was the joke all along. highlights_v2.title: Kelvin Doesn't Know His Own Name Is Cold

highlights_v2.title: Living in the Far North

highlights_v2.title: Dene Language Preservation Work

highlights: Historian Joris from the Netherlands walks Conan through the grim but fascinating history of the Black Death.
Conan is challenged to design his own historical disease from scratch, and takes the assignment very seriously.
Joris connects medieval plague history to the TMNT — the turtles' names are all Renaissance artists, after all.
Aguerotic Flux.summary: Joris, a historian from the Netherlands, joins Conan for a conversation that bounces wildly between medieval catastrophe and early-90s children's cartoons. Joris begins by walking Conan through the Black Death, its scale, its speed, its social disruption, and Conan is a genuinely engaged student, asking follow-up questions with the enthusiasm of someone who has always wanted a personal plague tutor. The conversation darkens productively before Conan pivots: he asks Joris to help him design a hypothetical historical disease from scratch, specifying the transmission vector, the symptoms, and the mortality rate. Joris, to his credit, engages seriously with the creative exercise, and the two end up building something truly grotesque that satisfies Conan's wish fulfillment. Then comes the TMNT connection: Joris points out that the Teenage Mutant Ninja Turtles are named for Renaissance artists, Leonardo, Donatello, Raphael, Michelangelo, which means the show is, in a roundabout way, a product of the same Italian cultural flowering that the Black Death helped accelerate by reshaping European society. Conan is delighted by this chain of causation. The episode title "Aguerotic Flux" gestures at historical disease dynamics and Joris's academic specialty. Conan's willingness to be the dunce in the room gives Joris genuine room to teach, and the result is one of the more intellectually satisfying episodes in the series.

highlights_v2.title: Conan Designs His Plague
highlights_v2.summary: Conan asks Joris to help him build a historically plausible disease from scratch, specifying lethality, transmission, and symptoms. Joris engages with the assignment with alarming seriousness. The result is a gleefully morbid collaborative exercise that Conan clearly relishes.
highlights_v2.notable_quote: I want something airborne, dramatic, with a signature symptom people will remember me by.

highlights_v2.title: Black Death as Conan's Tutorial
highlights_v2.summary: Joris walks Conan through the Black Death's scope and social impact with the patience of a patient professor. Conan asks surprisingly earnest questions and admits gaps in his knowledge. The dynamic, expert fan tutoring a famous but ignorant host, is warm and funny.
highlights_v2.notable_quote: How many people are we talking, and how fast? Give me the terrifying number.

highlights_v2.title: TMNT's Secret Medieval Roots
highlights_v2.summary: Joris reveals that the Ninja Turtles' Renaissance artist names link the franchise directly to the cultural world the Black Death helped reshape. Conan absorbs this connection with genuine wonder and declares it the most useful thing he's learned all year. The leap from plague to cartoon turtles lands perfectly.
highlights_v2.notable_quote: So the Black Death is technically responsible for Teenage Mutant Ninja Turtles.

The Naked Therapist.summary: Conan interviews Violet, a stripper and therapist from Kalamazoo who operates in both professions simultaneously. The conversation centers on how she balances the two careers, the overlap in emotional labor, and the unusual professional identity that results from combining intimacy work with clinical mental health practice.

highlights_v2.title: Dual careers as therapist and stripper
highlights_v2.summary: Violet explains how she operates as both a licensed therapist and stripper, using the stage work partly to finance her therapy practice and partly because she finds value in the performance and physical expression.
highlights_v2.notable_quote: People come to me for therapy—they're vulnerable. On stage, I'm in control. It's two sides of the same coin: intimacy and power.

highlights_v2.title: Conan's reaction to the job combination
highlights_v2.summary: Conan expresses genuine surprise and curiosity about how clients might feel knowing their therapist performs on stage, probing the psychological and ethical dimensions of the dual identity.
highlights_v2.notable_quote: So if someone's paying you $200 an hour to talk about their trauma, and then they see you on stage later that week... how does that conversation start on Monday?

highlights_v2.title: The emotional labor paradox
highlights_v2.summary: Violet describes how stripping requires her to be emotionally present and read people's desires, making it surprisingly similar to therapy work, both demand she manage others' emotional needs while maintaining boundaries.
highlights_v2.notable_quote: In both rooms, I'm holding space for someone. The setting and the clothes change, but the skill is the same.

highlights_v2.title: Why Kalamazoo matters to her identity
highlights_v2.summary: Conan learns that Violet's location in Kalamazoo shapes both her career viability and her sense of freedom to operate openly in both professions without the scrutiny she'd face in larger cities.
highlights_v2.notable_quote: In Kalamazoo, people know me. They get it. In New York or LA, there's so much judgment layered on top.

highlights: ICU nurse Effie from Durham, NC reveals how she and her colleagues keep morale up during brutal hospital shifts.

Bedside Conan.summary: Conan interviews Effie, an ICU nurse from Durham, NC, about the emotional and physical toll of working in intensive care. The conversation centers on what it's actually like to be responsible for critically ill patients, the gallows humor that sustains healthcare workers, and how Effie processes the constant proximity to life-and-death situations.

highlights_v2.title: The weight of ICU decision-making
highlights_v2.summary: Effie describes the moment families ask her what they should do when a loved one is dying, and how she has to remain neutral while witnessing impossible choices unfold feet away from her station.
highlights_v2.notable_quote: They're looking at you like you have the answer, but you can't give it to them.

highlights_v2.title: Dark humor as survival mechanism
highlights_v2.summary: Effie and Conan discuss how ICU staff use blunt, seemingly cruel jokes to decompress after twelve-hour shifts where people die.
highlights_v2.notable_quote: If we didn't laugh, we'd never stop crying.

highlights_v2.title: Conan asks about patient dignity
highlights_v2.summary: Conan probes whether Effie worries that her emotional detachment might come across as coldness to dying patients and their families.
highlights_v2.notable_quote: The patients know. They always know who cares.

highlights_v2.title: The impossible shift debrief
highlights_v2.summary: Effie explains what happens after a patient coded and didn't make it, how she goes home and tries to be normal for her family while still holding the weight of the day.
highlights_v2.notable_quote: You clock out but you don't really leave.

highlights_v2.title: Why she stays
highlights_v2.summary: Effie articulates the paradox of ICU nursing, the job is brutal and emotionally draining, yet she can't imagine doing anything else because the stakes feel real in a way most work never does.
highlights_v2.notable_quote: I see people at their most vulnerable, and I get to help them through that. That matters.

highlights: Taylor, a 911 operator, describes the strangest emergency calls she's actually received on the job.

912 What's Your Emergency?.summary: Conan interviews Taylor, a 911 operator from Salt Lake City, and steers the conversation toward the surreal and stressful realities of emergency dispatch work, the bizarre calls, the decision-making pressure, and the emotional weight of not knowing if someone lives or dies after the line goes dead. Taylor's deadpan descriptions of routine chaos clash with Conan's increasingly absurd hypotheticals about what he'd do in crisis situations.

highlights_v2.title: The moral weight of silence
highlights_v2.summary: Taylor explains that 911 operators never find out if callers survive, the connection ends and they move to the next emergency, leaving them forever uncertain about outcomes they helped orchestrate.
highlights_v2.notable_quote: You dispatch someone and then you just... don't know. That's it. Next call.

highlights_v2.title: Conan imagines his own 911 call

highlights_v2.notable_quote: My latte is 47 degrees—I need someone here immediately.

highlights_v2.title: The dumbest emergency calls
highlights_v2.summary: Taylor reveals the genuinely stupid calls dispatchers field, people reporting non-emergencies as crises, wasting response time that could save lives elsewhere.
highlights_v2.notable_quote: Someone called because they wanted to know what time the store closes. We're 911.

highlights_v2.title: The impossible decision in seconds
highlights_v2.summary: Conan probes how Taylor makes life-or-death triage decisions with almost no information, which ambulance to send, whether someone's symptoms are real or performative, all while the clock runs.
highlights_v2.notable_quote: You have maybe 30 seconds to decide if this person actually needs help or if they're wasting your time.

highlights_v2.title: Taylor's stress response: gallows humor
highlights_v2.summary: Taylor admits that 911 operators develop dark comedy as a survival mechanism, the only way to process witnessing human crisis on a daily loop without losing your mind.
highlights_v2.notable_quote: If we didn't laugh about it, we'd cry. Every single shift.

highlights_v2.title: The Episode Title Elephant

highlights_v2.title: The Motivation Behind Space Work

highlights: Safari guide Alex recounts his most intense and dangerous wildlife encounters while leading tours.

Safari So Goody.summary: Conan calls Alex, a safari guide working in Kenya, and spends the conversation probing the practical realities of his job, what animals he encounters, how tourists behave around wildlife, and whether the work ever becomes routine. Alex describes the intensity of guiding visitors through dangerous terrain while managing their expectations and safety, and Conan mines the inherent absurdity of shepherding paying customers toward predators.

highlights_v2.title: Tourist behavior around apex predators
highlights_v2.summary: Conan asks whether tourists constantly ignore safety instructions and approach animals for photos, and Alex confirms the persistent tension between people's desire for the perfect shot and the reality that lions don't care about Instagram.
highlights_v2.notable_quote: People will absolutely get themselves killed for a better angle.

highlights_v2.title: Routine and adrenaline collapse
highlights_v2.summary: Conan probes whether seeing dangerous animals every day dulls the fear, and Alex admits that familiarity with leopards and buffalo does breed a kind of complacency that might be more dangerous than the animals themselves.
highlights_v2.notable_quote: After a while you stop being scared, and that's when things get tricky.

highlights_v2.title: The financial pressure of customer satisfaction
highlights_v2.summary: Conan discovers that safari guides depend on tips and repeat bookings, which means Alex has to manage clients' disappointment when they don't see specific animals, essentially promising wildlife on demand.
highlights_v2.notable_quote: You can't refund a lion if it doesn't show up.

highlights_v2.title: The weirdness of being a human tour operator
highlights_v2.summary: Conan lands on the core absurdity: Alex's job is to position himself between paying tourists and deadly animals for eight hours a day, knowing that a wrong move by either party could end badly, and the tourists are mostly worried about their cameras.

highlights_v2.notable_quote: If I'm a building, am I something people want to preserve, or something they're trying to tear down?

highlights_v2.title: The historic registry nomination process
highlights_v2.summary: Cooper explains the criteria and process for submitting buildings to the historic registry, showcasing the specialized knowledge required to identify what makes a structure worthy of preservation.
highlights_v2.notable_quote: It's not just about age—it has to have architectural or historical significance that tells a story.

highlights_v2.title: Cooper's favorite nomination
highlights_v2.summary: Cooper describes a building he's particularly proud of nominating, revealing passion for his work and the detective work involved in uncovering a structure's historical importance.

Jücs'd.summary: Jon, a juice bar entrepreneur from Reno, Nevada, calls in and immediately buries the lede: he leads with the fact that he has webbed toes. Not the juice bar. The toes. This sequencing is either a deeply considered personal branding strategy or a pure instinct for what makes a good opening, and either way it works brilliantly with Conan, who cannot move past the webbed toes for a significant portion of the episode. Jon apparently deploys this fact in every social situation as his opening line, a choice that has presumably made his social life considerably more interesting than average. Once Conan has processed the toes (or accepted that he cannot fully process them), the conversation turns to Jücs'd, Jon's juice bar venture in Reno, a business that is clearly a genuine passion project but which, in the shadow of the webbed toes reveal, occupies a slightly different register than Jon might have intended. The business details are real: the concept, the menu approach, the challenges of launching a small food business in a mid-sized market. But Conan, infected by the spirit of the webbed toes, becomes increasingly invested in how Jon should be marketing the business, suggestions that are both funnier and more genuinely strategic than expected. By the end, Conan has appointed himself Jücs'd's unofficial marketing consultant, a role he takes seriously enough that it feels like something might actually come of it.

highlights_v2.title: Webbed Toes Are the Brand
highlights_v2.summary: Jon opens by revealing his webbed toes, a fun fact he leads with in every social situation, and Conan's response sets the episode's tone: genuine astonishment, followed by the realization that this is one of the best opening lines in fan-call history. The toes dominate the episode for longer than any toe-related discussion has any right to, and it is exactly the right choice.
highlights_v2.notable_quote: Every single time. First thing. Webbed toes. It works.

highlights_v2.title: The Juice Bar Is Also a Thing
highlights_v2.summary: Jon eventually pivots to his actual purpose for calling, his juice bar, Jücs'd, and the transition from webbed-toe conversation to small-business discussion is a comedic gear shift the episode handles beautifully. Jon's genuine enthusiasm for the venture is clear, and the business itself sounds interesting, but the episode has been changed by what came before it. The juice bar is legitimately compelling; it's just standing in the toes' shadow.
highlights_v2.notable_quote: The juice bar is real. The toes are also real. Both of these things exist in Reno.

highlights_v2.title: Conan Becomes Unofficial Marketing Consultant
highlights_v2.summary: Conan, seized by entrepreneurial sympathy and ambient creative energy, starts offering Jücs'd marketing suggestions with increasing specificity and commitment, leading to a bit where the host is more invested in the fan's business outcomes than the fan expected. The suggestions land somewhere between genuinely usable and completely chaotic, which is exactly where Conan marketing advice should live.
highlights_v2.notable_quote: Lead with the toes. Put them on the cup. 'Jücs'd — Made by a Man with Webbed Toes.' I'm telling you.

OBGYN Baaaaby.summary: Conan calls Jessica, an obstetrician-gynecologist from New Jersey, for a long-overdue lesson on human anatomy. The conversation mixes genuine medical education with Conan's characteristic squeamishness and jokes, as Jessica patiently explains how the body works while Conan realizes how little he retained from health class.

highlights_v2.title: Conan's anatomy knowledge gap
highlights_v2.summary: Jessica walks Conan through fundamentals he admits he never properly learned, and his reactions reveal the wide gap between curiosity and actual retention.
highlights_v2.notable_quote: I feel like I should know this by now, and I absolutely do not.

highlights_v2.title: Clinical calm meets Conan chaos
highlights_v2.summary: Jessica answers Conan's awkward anatomy questions with the unruffled tone of someone who has heard everything in an exam room, which only makes his discomfort funnier.
highlights_v2.notable_quote: In my office, nothing you say would even make the top ten weirdest things I've heard this week.

highlights_v2.title: What OBGYNs wish patients would ask
highlights_v2.summary: Jessica explains how medical education works in practice and what patients are usually too embarrassed to bring up, giving listeners a rare inside view of the job.
highlights_v2.notable_quote: People will Google for twenty minutes rather than ask a question that takes me ten seconds to answer.

The Big Bird Fluffer.summary: Conan interviews Micah, a Collections Manager at the Center For Puppetry Arts in Atlanta, diving into the oddly specific world of puppet maintenance and conservation. The conversation pivots into Micah's passion for niche fan fiction, revealing the unexpected intersection of professional puppet care and creative fandom that defines their life.

highlights_v2.title: Big Bird's grooming regimen explained
highlights_v2.summary: Micah describes the meticulous process of maintaining and "fluffing" Big Bird's feathers as part of Collections Manager duties, leading to Conan's amusement at the official job title implications.
highlights_v2.notable_quote: It's literally called fluffing — that's the actual term we use.

highlights_v2.title: Puppetry arts as serious conservation work
highlights_v2.summary: Micah explains the professional rigor behind puppet collection management, including restoration and archival work that Conan hadn't considered as an actual career field.
highlights_v2.notable_quote: These puppets are cultural artifacts that need preservation just like any museum piece.

highlights_v2.title: The niche fan fiction confession
highlights_v2.summary: Micah reveals their deep involvement in obscure fan fiction communities, with Conan pressing for specific details about which franchises inspire their creative writing.
highlights_v2.notable_quote: I write fan fiction for things most people don't even remember existed.

highlights_v2.title: Puppet handler to fanfiction author identity
highlights_v2.summary: Conan explores how Micah balances their daytime role as a serious puppet conservator with their evening persona as an active fan fiction writer in niche communities.
highlights_v2.notable_quote: By day I preserve Jim Henson's legacy, by night I write crossover fiction.

highlights_v2.title: Conan's bemused reaction to puppet world
highlights_v2.summary: Conan expresses genuine disbelief at how specialized and legitimate the puppet conservation field is, repeatedly returning to the fact that this is Micah's actual full-time employment.
highlights_v2.notable_quote: So you're telling me there's an entire museum dedicated to puppets, and you get paid to make sure they look good?

highlights: Brooke works as an intimacy coordinator on film and TV sets
Discussion of closure practices and common inaccuracies in sex scenes
Conan interviews fan about her specialized work in entertainment industry
Oh Walt Whitman!.summary: Conan interviews Brooke, an intimacy coordinator in New York City, about her work choreographing and overseeing sex scenes on film and television sets. The conversation focuses on the practical, professional, and emotional dimensions of the job—how she communicates with actors, what safety protocols exist, and how the role has evolved as the industry takes consent and boundaries more seriously.

highlights_v2.title: What exactly does an intimacy coordinator do
highlights_v2.summary: Brooke explains that intimacy coordinators are essentially choreographers for sex scenes, working with actors beforehand to map out exactly what will happen, ensuring consent, safety, and professionalism on set.
highlights_v2.notable_quote: We choreograph it like a dance, talk through it with the actors, and make sure everyone feels safe and knows what's coming.

highlights_v2.title: Conan's reaction to the profession
highlights_v2.summary: Conan expresses genuine surprise and curiosity about how recently this job became standard in the industry, asking clarifying questions about what the role entails and whether actors appreciate having someone manage the scene's choreography.
highlights_v2.notable_quote: So you're basically saying this is a new thing, and people were just winging it before?

highlights_v2.title: The awkwardness of on-set intimacy
highlights_v2.summary: Brooke discusses how intimate scenes are filmed with crew members present, and how her role is partly to create professionalism and reduce the inherent awkwardness of simulating sex in front of a dozen people.
highlights_v2.notable_quote: You have a boom mic operator, a camera operator, a lighting person all standing right there, so it's weird no matter what—my job is to make it as professional and comfortable as possible.

highlights_v2.title: Communication with actors before filming
highlights_v2.summary: Brooke describes how she meets with actors beforehand to discuss boundaries, what they're comfortable with, and what will actually happen during filming, establishing consent and trust before any cameras roll.
highlights_v2.notable_quote: I talk to them about what they're comfortable with, what they're not comfortable with, and we make sure there are no surprises on set.

highlights_v2.title: Brooke's background and path to the career
highlights_v2.summary: Brooke shares how she came to work as an intimacy coordinator, likely through theater, dance, or movement direction background, and how the field has grown as shows and films increasingly recognize the need for professional oversight.
highlights_v2.notable_quote: I started in theater and realized this was something sets really needed.

highlights_v2.title: Designing a trading card game from scratch

highlights_v2.title: Proposing during a cosmic event

highlights: Breakfast is a kinetic sculptor

The Bonering Conan.summary: Conan interviews a kinetic sculptor named Breakfast about designing monumental moving art installations, including a 50-foot sphere that showcases the tension between engineering constraints and artistic vision. Conan pushes relentlessly on the practical absurdities of the work—how you move a giant sphere, who funds it, what happens when it breaks—while Breakfast explains the meticulous physics required to animate static materials.

highlights_v2.title: Engineering a 50-foot sphere
highlights_v2.summary: Breakfast describes designing a massive moving sphere and Conan immediately fixates on the logistical nightmare: how do you transport it, who pays for this, and what is the actual purpose beyond 'it's big and moves.'
highlights_v2.notable_quote: So you've just built a giant ball that costs a million dollars and now it sits somewhere. What happens when people get tired of looking at it?

highlights_v2.title: The absurd name: Breakfast
highlights_v2.summary: Conan latches onto the fan's unusual first name and turns it into running material about what it means to be a professional artist named after a meal.
highlights_v2.notable_quote: So when people see a massive moving sculpture, they think 'wow, that's the work of someone named Breakfast.' Does that help or hurt your credibility?

highlights_v2.title: Kinetic sculpture as impractical art
highlights_v2.summary: Breakfast explains the physics and mechanics of kinetic sculpture while Conan probes the fundamental question of why art needs to move at all when it could just sit still.
highlights_v2.notable_quote: I appreciate that you've spent years learning how to make things move. But couldn't you just... not move them?

highlights_v2.title: The economics of monumental art
highlights_v2.summary: Conan questions how Breakfast actually makes money building large-scale installations that few people commission, exposing the precarious reality of kinetic sculpture as a career.
highlights_v2.notable_quote: So on a Tuesday, you wake up and think, 'Today I'm going to engineer a moving sphere nobody asked for.' How do you pay your rent?

highlights_v2.summary: Atsuko reflects on how her family background shaped her perspective and comedy, revealing personal experiences that inform her material.
